<output>Hello, World!</output>
In diraclang, if num is greater than 10, print 'Value is large'; otherwise, print 'Value is small'.
<defvar name="num" value="5" />  <test-if test="$num > 10">    <output>Value is large</output>  </test-if>  <test-if test="$num <= 10">    <output>Value is small</output>  </test-if>
In diraclang, loop from 0 to 2 and print each value.
<defvar name="i" value="0" />  <loop count="3">    <output><variable name="i" /></output>    <assign name="i"><eval><variable name="i" /> + 1</eval></assign>  </loop>
In diraclang, define a variable myvar with value 'test123' and print it.
<defvar name="myvar" value="test123" />  <output>Value is: <variable name="myvar" /> </output>
In diraclang, define variables a=10, b=5, and print their sum as 'Result: 15'.
<defvar name="a" value="10" />  <defvar name="b" value="5" />  <output>Result: </output>  <expr eval="plus">    <arg><variable name="a" /></arg>    <arg><variable name="b" /></arg>  </expr>
In diraclang, define a variable x=5, use an if conditional to check if x equals 5, print 'Match!' if true, 'No match!' if false.

<defvar name="x" value="5" />  <if>    <cond eval="eq">      <arg><variable name="x" /></arg>      <arg>5</arg>    </cond>    <then>      <output>Match!</output>    </then>    <else>      <output>No match!</output>    </else>  </if>
In diraclang, throw an error and catch it, then print 'Caught error'.
<try>    <eval>throw new Error("Test error");</eval>  </try>  <catch>    <output>Caught error</output>  </catch>
In diraclang, write two lines to a file, output to stdout, verify file and clean up.

<output file="output-test-temp.txt">File line 1</output>  <output file="output-test-temp.txt">File line 2</output>  <output>stdout output</output>  <system shell="bash">    if [ -f output-test-temp.txt ]; then      echo "File written"      rm output-test-temp.txt    fi  </system>
In diraclang, import a math library and call SQUARE with x=5.
<import src="../examples/lib/math.di" />  <call name="SQUARE" x="5" />
In diraclang, define a subroutine fullname with first and last name, and print 'John Doe'.

<subroutine name="fullname" param-first="string" param-last="string">    <eval name="result">return first + ' ' + last;</eval>    <output><variable name="result" /></output>  </subroutine>    <call name="fullname" first="John" last="Doe" />
In diraclang, define a parent subroutine without visible, call nested, and print cleanup message.

<subroutine name="PARENT_WITHOUT_VISIBLE">  <output>Parent without visible called</output>    <subroutine name="SHOULD_BE_CLEANED">    <output>This should not be called</output>  </subroutine> </subroutine>  <PARENT_WITHOUT_VISIBLE />  <output>After call: nested was cleaned up</output>

In diraclang, generate a script, write to file, execute, and print result.

<defvar name="generated_script" literal="true" trim="false">
<defvar name="value">42</defvar>  <output>Result: <variable name="value" /></output>  </defvar>  <output file="temp-generated.di"><variable name="generated_script" /></output>  <output>Generated file executed</output>  <system shell="bash">    node dist/cli.js temp-generated.di    rm temp-generated.di  </system>

In diraclang, import a library with variable substitution and double 10.

<subroutine name="TestSuite">    <defvar name="libPath">./fixtures/test-lib.di</defvar>    <import src="${libPath}" />    <defvar name="result" trim="true"><DOUBLE x="10" /></defvar>    <output><variable name="result" /></output>  </subroutine>  <TestSuite />
In diraclang, set background color to blue using llm and validate.

<subroutine name="set-color" description="Set a background color" param-color="string:required:Color name">    <parameters select="@color"/>    <output>Background color is: <variable name="color" /></output>  </subroutine>  <llm execute="true" validate="true" maxTokens="100">    Set the background color to blue  </llm>
In diraclang, break a loop at 3 and print after loop.

<loop count="10">  <test-if test="$i" ge="3">    <output>Breaking at <variable name="i" /></output>    <break />  </test-if>  <output>Loop: <variable name="i" /></output> </loop> <output>After loop</output>

In diraclang, Create a nested loop: for i in 0..1 and j in 0..1, print i and letter A or B depending on j.

<loop count="2" var="i">    <loop count="2" var="j">      <eval name="letter">return j === 0 ? 'A' : 'B';</eval>      <output><variable name="i" />-<variable name="letter" /> </output>    </loop>  </loop>
In diraclang, Define parent and child subroutines, child extends parent and overrides nested, call child and print outputs.

<subroutine name="parent">    <subroutine name="nested">      <output>Parent nested</output>    </subroutine>    <output>In parent, calling: </output>    <call name="nested" />  </subroutine>    <subroutine name="child" extends="parent">    <subroutine name="nested">      <output>Child nested</output>    </subroutine>  </subroutine>    <call name="child" />
In diraclang, Define a TestSuite subroutine with age, gender, greet, and use available-subroutines to list them.

In diraclang, Define inner and outer subroutines, call outer with p=x and q=y, and print main scope variables before and after.

<subroutine name="inner" param-r="string" param-s="string">    <output>inner: r=<variable name="r" /> s=<variable name="s" /></output>  </subroutine>  <subroutine name="outer" param-p="string" param-q="string">    <output>outer: p=<variable name="p" /> q=<variable name="q" /></output>    <inner r="$p" s="$q" />    <output>outer after inner: p=<variable name="p" /> q=<variable name="q" /></output>  </subroutine>  <defvar name="a">x</defvar>  <defvar name="b">y</defvar>  <defvar name="c">z</defvar>  <output>main: a=<variable name="a" /> b=<variable name="b" /> c=<variable name="c" /></output>  <outer p="$a" q="$b" />  <output>main after outer: a=<variable name="a" /> b=<variable name="b" /> c=<variable name="c" /></output>
In diraclang, Define base subroutine with age and gender, extend base to override age, and print age and gender.

<subroutine name="base">    <subroutine name="age">      <output>60</output>    </subroutine>    <subroutine name="gender">      <output>M</output>    </subroutine>    <parameters select="*" />  </subroutine>  <subroutine name="base" extends="base">    <subroutine name="age">      <output>63</output>    </subroutine>    <parameters select="*" />  </subroutine>  <base><age /></base>  <output>,</output>  <base><gender /></base>
In diraclang, Start a background process that counts, check file lines in a loop, verify completion, and clean up.

<output>Testing background processes with loop...</output>  <system>rm -f /tmp/dirac-bg-counter.txt</system>  <system background="true">(for i in 1 2 3 4 5; do echo "Count: $i"; sleep 1; done > /tmp/dirac-bg-counter.txt) &</system>  <output>✓ Background counter started</output>  <output>✓ DIRAC continuing without blocking</output>  <system>sleep 2</system>  <loop count="3">    <system>wc -l /tmp/dirac-bg-counter.txt 2>/dev/null || echo "0"</system>    <output>Check: Background file now has lines</output>    <system>sleep 1</system>  </loop>  <system>sleep 2</system>  <system>wc -l /tmp/dirac-bg-counter.txt</system>  <output>✓ Background process completed</output>  <system>grep -c "Count:" /tmp/dirac-bg-counter.txt</system>  <output>✓ Found all count entries</output>  <system>rm -f /tmp/dirac-bg-counter.txt</system>  <output>✓ Background process with loop test passed</output>

Describe the Dirac language.

Dirac is a declarative execution language designed for the AI era, where large language models act as autonomous agents. It bridges human-readable instructions with machine execution, allowing LLMs to generate, execute, and recursively call code for complex tasks.

Dirac is a declarative execution language designed for the AI era, where large language models act as autonomous agents. It bridges human-readable instructions with machine execution, allowing LLMs to generate, execute, and recursively call code for complex tasks.

Dirac is an XML-based language for defining and calling subroutines.

Example:
```xml
<subroutine name="greet" param-name="string">
  <output>Hello, <variable name="name"/>!</output>
</subroutine>
<greet name="Alice" />
```

Available tags:
- defvar: Define a new variable with optional visibility
- assign: Assign new value to existing variable
- variable: Retrieve and output variable value
- environment: Read environment variable value
- output: Emit content to stdout or file
- input: Read from stdin or file
- if: Conditional execution with cond/then/else children
- test-if: Attribute-based conditional execution
- loop: Iterate fixed number of times
- foreach: Iterate over XML elements
- break: Exit current loop or foreach iteration
- expr: Arithmetic and logical operations
- eval: Evaluate JavaScript expression with full context
- subroutine: Define reusable code block
- call: Invoke defined subroutine
- parameters: Access parameters passed to subroutine
- available-subroutines: List all registered subroutines as XML
- list-subroutines: List subroutines in specified format
- save-subroutine: Save subroutine definition to disk
- edit-subroutine: Edit subroutine definition in external editor
- subroutine-index: Search and manage subroutine knowledge base
- index-subroutines: Index subroutines from files or directories for later search
- search-subroutines: Search indexed subroutines by name or description
- execute: Execute dynamically generated DIRAC code
- system: Execute shell commands
- import: Import subroutines from other DIRAC files
- require_module: Load Node.js module into variable
- llm: Invoke Large Language Model with validation
- load-context: Load subroutine definitions as LLM context
- try: Establish exception boundary
- catch: Catch exceptions by name
- throw: Throw named exception
- exception: Access caught exception content
- attr: Extract attribute from XML element
- schedule: Run tasks on interval without blocking
- cron: Run tasks on cron schedule without blocking
- run-at: Run task once at future time without blocking
- mongodb: MongoDB database operations
- tag-check: Validate tag name availability
- ai: AI assistant with context loading (internal wrapper - use llm tag directly instead)

Create a Dirac subroutine to list directory contents

<subroutine name="ls"
  description="List directory contents"
  param-path="string:optional:Directory to list (default current directory):/tmp"
  param-all="boolean:optional:Show hidden files"
  param-long="boolean:optional:Use long listing format"
  meta-bash-command="ls">

  <defvar name="cmd" value="ls" />
  <test-if test="$all" eq="true">
    <assign name="cmd"><variable name="cmd" /> -a</assign>
  </test-if>
  <test-if test="$long" eq="true">
    <assign name="cmd"><variable name="cmd" /> -l</assign>
  </test-if>
  <test-if test="$path" ne="">
    <assign name="cmd"><variable name="cmd" /> <variable name="path" /></assign>
  </test-if>

  <system><variable name="cmd" /></system>
</subroutine>
